attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll perform the Refactor phase. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` → `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
26	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver — the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 → ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body — both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100×1.5×1.3×1.1 = 214.5 → round→215, ceil→215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 × 0.80 × 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil → 53, +5 = 58 — also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the round→ceil switch until a test actually fails.
27	- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
31	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8–9 (loyalty discount: `100 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 × 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil → 28) — every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
32	- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
36

content.text:
The most recent test (cycle 9, amulet with loyal customer, premium 58) passed with no code change — the loyalty ternary added in cycle 8 plus `ceilInMhpcoFavor` from cycle 7 handled it. Let me evaluate refactoring opportunities.

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:
- `runScenario` — still fits the contract (drives a parsed scenario object); keep.
- `itemPrice` — accurate (base × item-modifiers); keep.
- `loyaltyMultiplier` — clear; keep.
- `itemModifierMultiplier` — describes its scope (item-attribute factors only); keep.
- `ceilInMhpcoFavor` — encodes the spec's domain phrase; keep.

**Current Code Mass (runScenario body, lines 33–43)**:
- Constants: 2 (literal `0`, `1`, `1`) — wait recounting: `0` in `[0]` twice, `[0]` twice = 2 literals + `2` in `>= 2` + `1` for fallback = ~5. Plus property keys. Roughly **5**.
- Bindings: `customer`, `steps`, `item`, `itemPrice`, `loyaltyMultiplier`, `premium`, `scenario`, plus property accesses ~ **15**.
- Invocations: cast, `BASE_PRICE[...]`, `itemModifierMultiplier(item)`, `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)`, multiplications (×3), addition (×1) = ~ **8** → mass 16.
- Conditionals: 1 ternary = 4.
- Loops: 0.
- Assignments: 6 `const` declarations = 6 (or count as bindings — bindings rule).

Rough total: ~38–42.

**Refactoring Candidates**:

Looking at cycle-8's architecture note: "Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver." Cycle 9 added no new customer-side modifier (just exercised loyalty + ceil divergence). So that judgment still holds — still only one customer-side modifier.

Looking at the next pending tests:
- Rune (cycle 10): adds `rune: 25` to BASE_PRICE, no new logic.
- Three runes form building block: introduces multi-item grouping logic.
- Sword + amulet: multi-item sum.
- Two-quote scenario: introduces post-first-contract discount (0.85) AND multiple steps.

The post-first-contract discount is the second customer-side modifier — but it's quote-index-dependent, not pure customer-side. Cycle-8's note explicitly warns: "extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature."

So customer-side extraction is still premature.

**What about the comment on `ceilInMhpcoFavor`?** It's good documentation. **What about the type cast `scenario as Scenario`?** That's structural and would need a real validator to remove — premature.

**What about extracting the premium pipeline?** The current `runScenario` body has three stages:
1. Destructure inputs (lines 34–35)
2. Compute itemPrice with item modifiers (line 36)
3. Compute customer-side loyalty (lines 37–38)
4. Apply first-insurance loading + admin fee, with ceil (lines 39–41)

Could extract `quotePremium(item, customer): number`. But: next test (rune cycle 10) is absorbed by BASE_PRICE alone (zero edits). Then the building-block tests will force a `quotePremium(items: Item[], customer)` signature change. Extracting now with single-item signature would lock in the wrong shape and need rework — same Rule 4 argument as cycle 8.

**One small Rule 2 improvement**: the `>= 2` magic number in the loyalty ternary. The spec says "loyal customer = ≥2 years with MHPCO". Could extract `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2` to mirror the constant convention, OR extract `isLoyal(customer)` predicate. The constant approach is lighter and matches the established `*_MULTIPLIER` pattern (sibling constants describe pricing parameters).

Let me apply this small but real improvement.

content.text: All 9 tests still pass. Now append the architecture note.

input.old_string:
## After cycle 8
- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
input.new_string:
## After cycle 8
- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).

## After cycle 9
- Change: extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`; replaced magic `>= 2` in the loyalty ternary with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline — every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. The spec's domain phrase "loyal customer ≥2 years" now reads in the code. Mass impact negligible (+1 binding, -1 constant from inline literal moving to named binding). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (cycle-8 reasoning unchanged — the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature, so extracting now locks in the wrong shape). Cycle 10 (rune 33) is a pure `BASE_PRICE` table addition — zero edits to `runScenario` predicted.
- Naming established: numeric thresholds that gate a multiplier follow the `*_THRESHOLD` suffix (sibling to `*_MULTIPLIER`); both stand together to read as "if customer crosses THRESHOLD, apply MULTIPLIER".

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Naming Evaluation**: All names still fit (`runScenario`, `itemPrice`, `loyaltyMultiplier`, `itemModifierMultiplier`, `ceilInMhpcoFavor`). No renames needed.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`
- Replaced inline magic `>= 2` with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` in the loyalty ternary
- Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline; every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant. The spec's "loyal customer ≥2 years" phrase now reads in the code.

**Mass Change**: ~41 → ~41 (binding +1, inline constant -1, net zero; clarity gained).

**Tests**: 9 passed, 15 todo (unchanged).

**Resisted** (per cycle-8 reasoning, still valid):
- Extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` — the upcoming two-quote test (cycle 15) will force a non-customer-only signature (post-first-contract discount depends on quote index, not just customer), so extracting now locks in the wrong shape (Rule 4).
- Extracting `quotePremium(item, customer)` — the building-block tests (cycles 11-13) will force a multi-item signature, same issue.

**Files**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md`

Proceeding to the next test.
